infobox.value: 1. [North Warwickshire](/wiki/North_Warwickshire "North Warwickshire") 2. [Nuneaton and Bedworth](/wiki/Nuneaton_and_Bedworth "Nuneaton and Bedworth") 3. [Rugby](/wiki/Borough_of_Rugby "Borough of Rugby") 4. [Stratford-on-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-on-Avon_District "Stratford-on-Avon District") 5. [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick_District "Warwick District")

([/ˈwɒrɪkʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-warwickshire.ogg "File:En-warwickshire.ogg"); abbreviated **Warks**) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") of England. It is bordered by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") and [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") to the north, [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") to the east, [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") to the south, and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") to the west. The largest settlement is [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton").

**Warwickshire** ([/ˈwɒrɪkʃər, -ʃɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-warwickshire.ogg "File:En-warwickshire.ogg"); abbreviated **Warks**) is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") of England. It is bordered by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") and [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") to the north, [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") to the east, [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire") and [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") to the south, and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") to the west. The largest settlement is [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton").

The county is largely rural, with an area of 763 sq mi (1,980 km2) and an estimated population of 632,207 in 2024. It contains a number of towns, including Nuneaton and [Bedworth](/wiki/Bedworth "Bedworth") in the north-east, [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire") in the east, [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon") in the south-west, and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") and [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa") adjacent to each other in the centre. For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes, Warwickshire is a [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") with five [districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district"). The county [historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") included the city of [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and the area to its west, including [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Royal_Town_of_Sutton_Coldfield "Royal Town of Sutton Coldfield"), [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") and the [city centre](/wiki/Birmingham_city_centre "Birmingham city centre") of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham").

Warwickshire is a flat, lowland county, but its far south contains part of the [Cotswolds](/wiki/Cotswolds "Cotswolds"), which have been designated a [national landscape](/wiki/National_landscape "National landscape"). The [River Avon](/wiki/River_Avon,_Warwickshire "River Avon, Warwickshire"), a major tributary of the [Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn"), flows through the south of the county.

The region was part of [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") and later the Roman road called [Watling Street](/wiki/Watling_Street "Watling Street") became the boundary between the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") kingdom of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") and the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw"). The county was relatively settled during the rest of the Middle Ages and Early Modern period; Coventry developed as a major centre of the textiles trade. The playwright [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") was born in [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon") in 1564, living much of his life there, and the [Gunpowder Plot](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot") of 1605 was planned near [Snitterfield](/wiki/Snitterfield "Snitterfield"). During the Industrial Revolution, the [Warwickshire coalfield](/wiki/Warwickshire_Coalfield "Warwickshire Coalfield") was exploited and Coventry and the west of the county became manufacturing centres; Leamington Spa developed as a tourist resort at the same time. The Victorian novelist Mary Ann Evans, better known as [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot"), was born just outside Nuneaton in 1819.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Warwickshire is bordered by [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") to the northeast, [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") to the northwest, [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") to the west, [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") to the east and southeast, [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") to the southwest and [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire") to the south. The northern tip of the county is only 3 miles (5 km) from the [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire") border. An average-sized English county covering an area of 1,975 km2 (760 sq mi),[[5]](#cite_note-5) it runs some 56 miles (90 km) north to south.

The majority of Warwickshire's population live in the north and centre of the county.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The market towns of northern and eastern Warwickshire were industrialised in the 19th century, and include [Atherstone](/wiki/Atherstone "Atherstone"), [Bedworth](/wiki/Bedworth "Bedworth"), [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill,_Warwickshire "Coleshill, Warwickshire"), [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton"), and [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire"). Major industries included [coal mining](/wiki/UK_Coal "UK Coal"), [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") and [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement") production, but heavy industry is in decline, being replaced by distribution centres, light to medium industry and services. Of the northern and eastern towns, Nuneaton and Rugby (as the birthplace of [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football")) are best known outside of Warwickshire. The prosperous towns of central and western Warwickshire, including [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa"), [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick"), [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon"), [Kenilworth](/wiki/Kenilworth "Kenilworth"), [Alcester](/wiki/Alcester "Alcester"), [Southam](/wiki/Southam "Southam") and [Wellesbourne](/wiki/Wellesbourne "Wellesbourne"), harbour tourism, gaming and services as major employment sectors.

The north of the county, bordering Staffordshire and Leicestershire, is mildly undulating countryside (rising to 178m / 581 ft near [Hartshill](/wiki/Hartshill "Hartshill")) and the northernmost village, [No Man's Heath](/wiki/No_Man%27s_Heath,_Warwickshire "No Man's Heath, Warwickshire"), is only 34 miles (55 km) south of the [Peak District National Park](/wiki/Peak_District_National_Park "Peak District National Park")'s southernmost point.

The south of the county is largely rural and sparsely populated, and includes a very small area of the [Cotswolds](/wiki/Cotswolds "Cotswolds"), at the border with northeast Gloucestershire. The plain between the outlying Cotswolds and the [Edgehill](/wiki/Edge_Hill,_Warwickshire "Edge Hill, Warwickshire") escarpment is known as the [Vale of Red Horse](/wiki/Vale_of_Red_Horse "Vale of Red Horse").[[7]](#cite_note-pcnfc-7) The only [town](/wiki/Town "Town") in the south of Warwickshire is [Shipston-on-Stour](/wiki/Shipston-on-Stour "Shipston-on-Stour"). The highest point in the county, at 261 m (856 ft), is [Ebrington Hill](/wiki/Ebrington_Hill "Ebrington Hill"), again on the border with Gloucestershire, [grid reference](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey_National_Grid "Ordnance Survey National Grid") [SP187426](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Warwickshire&params=52.081445_N_1.728531_W_region:GB_scale:25000) at the county's southwest extremity.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

There are no cities in Warwickshire since both [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") were incorporated into the West Midlands county in 1974 and are now metropolitan authorities in themselves. According to the [2021 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the largest towns (+20,000 pop.) in Warwickshire were: Nuneaton (pop. 88,815), Rugby (78,120), Leamington Spa (51,310), Bedworth (31,090), Warwick (36,680), Stratford-upon-Avon (28,120), and Kenilworth (22,235).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Arden and Felden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Arden and Felden")]

Much of western Warwickshire, including the area now forming part of Coventry, Solihull and Birmingham, was covered by the ancient [Forest of Arden](/wiki/Forest_of_Arden "Forest of Arden")[[11]](#cite_note-11) (most of which was cut down to provide fuel for industrialisation). Thus the names of a number of places in the central-western part of Warwickshire end with the phrase "-in-Arden", such as [Henley-in-Arden](/wiki/Henley-in-Arden "Henley-in-Arden"), [Hampton-in-Arden](/wiki/Hampton-in-Arden "Hampton-in-Arden") and [Tanworth-in-Arden](/wiki/Tanworth-in-Arden "Tanworth-in-Arden"). The remaining area, not part of the forest, was called the Felden – from *[fielden](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fielden "wiktionary:fielden") -* and is now an undulating and agricultural landscape, through which the rivers Avon and Leam flow.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Historic county boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historic county boundaries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Birmingham-Skyline-from-the-West.jpg/250px-Birmingham-Skyline-from-the-West.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birmingham-Skyline-from-the-West.jpg)

Much of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), presently the UK's second-largest city, was historically in Warwickshire

Areas [historically part](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") of Warwickshire include [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull"), [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield"), and much of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") including the [city centre](/wiki/Birmingham_city_centre "Birmingham city centre"), [Aston](/wiki/Aston "Aston"), [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich"), [Digbeth](/wiki/Digbeth "Digbeth"), [Edgbaston](/wiki/Edgbaston "Edgbaston"), [Erdington](/wiki/Erdington "Erdington"), [Hodge Hill](/wiki/Hodge_Hill "Hodge Hill"), [Ladywood](/wiki/Ladywood "Ladywood"), [Nechells](/wiki/Nechells "Nechells"), [Saltley](/wiki/Saltley "Saltley"), [Shard End](/wiki/Shard_End "Shard End"), [Sheldon](/wiki/Sheldon,_Warwickshire "Sheldon, Warwickshire"), [Small Heath](/wiki/Small_Heath "Small Heath") and [Sparkbrook](/wiki/Sparkbrook "Sparkbrook"). Other areas historically in Warwickshire, all now in the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") following [local government re-organisation in 1974](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), include [Marston Green](/wiki/Marston_Green "Marston Green"), [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_Warwickshire "Meriden, Warwickshire"), [Chelmsley Wood](/wiki/Chelmsley_Wood "Chelmsley Wood"), [Kingshurst](/wiki/Kingshurst "Kingshurst"), and [Smith's Wood](/wiki/Smith%27s_Wood "Smith's Wood").

In 1986 the [West Midlands County Council](/wiki/West_Midlands_County_Council "West Midlands County Council") was abolished and Birmingham, Coventry, and Solihull became effective [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"). However, the West Midlands county name has not been altogether abolished, and still exists for [ceremonial purposes](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England"). Since 2016, it has been used as part of the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority"), with powers over transport, economic development and regeneration. Some organisations, such as [Warwickshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Warwickshire_County_Cricket_Club "Warwickshire County Cricket Club"), which is based in [Edgbaston](/wiki/Edgbaston "Edgbaston"), in Birmingham, observe the [historic county](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") boundaries.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The [flag of the historic county](/wiki/Flag_of_Warwickshire "Flag of Warwickshire") was registered in October 2016. It is a design of a [bear and ragged staff](/wiki/Bear_and_Ragged_Staff "Bear and Ragged Staff") on a red field, which is long associated with the county.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Flag_of_Warwickshire.svg/250px-Flag_of_Warwickshire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Warwickshire.svg)

The flag of the historic county of Warwickshire

[Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") is effectively in the centre of the Warwickshire area, and still has strong ties with the county. Coventry and Warwickshire are sometimes treated as a single area and share a single [Chamber of Commerce](/wiki/Chamber_of_Commerce "Chamber of Commerce"), [Local Enterprise Partnership](/wiki/Local_enterprise_partnership "Local enterprise partnership") and BBC Local Radio Station ([BBC CWR](/wiki/BBC_CWR "BBC CWR")).

[Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") was administered separately from the rest of Warwickshire between 1451 and 1842. It formed the [County of the City of Coventry](/wiki/County_of_the_City_of_Coventry "County of the City of Coventry"), a [county corporate](/wiki/County_corporate "County corporate") from 1451. In 1842 the county corporate of Coventry was abolished and remerged with the rest of Warwickshire.

The town of [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire") was historically divided between Warwickshire and [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), but since 1888 has been fully in Staffordshire.

### Green belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Green belt")]

Main article: [West Midlands Green Belt](/wiki/West_Midlands_Green_Belt "West Midlands Green Belt")

Warwickshire contains a large expanse of [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom) "Green belt (United Kingdom)") area, surrounding the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation") and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry/Bedworth_Urban_Area "Coventry/Bedworth Urban Area") conurbations, and was first drawn up from the 1950s.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] All the county's districts contain some portion of the belt.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economy")]

Warwickshire has a strong and growing economy with the automotive industry being a major contributor. In the north, BMW's Hams Hall plant employs over 1,000 people,[[16]](#cite_note-16) while Jaguar Land Rover and Aston Martin Lagonda have headquarters, including a giant advanced production creation centre,[[17]](#cite_note-17) at Gaydon in the south.

Warwickshire is also establishing a growing reputation as a global hub of the [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry").[[18]](#cite_note-18) One of Britain's oldest still-running game studios, [Codemasters](/wiki/Codemasters "Codemasters"), has operated out of [Southam](/wiki/Southam "Southam") for decades; the greater "[Silicon Spa](/wiki/List_of_places_with_%27Silicon%27_names "List of places with 'Silicon' names")"[[19]](#cite_note-19) area, including Southam, [Royal Leamington Spa](/wiki/Royal_Leamington_Spa "Royal Leamington Spa") and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick"), is now home to dozens of game studios which employ a combined total of over 2,000 highly skilled people, equating to more than 10% of the UK's games development workforce.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Increasingly the region is establishing itself as one of the leading areas in battery technology with major developments announced in 2021 that include a £130 million UK Battery Industrialisation Centre (UKBIC)[[21]](#cite_note-21) based in Coventry.

Tourism is also a key area of employment with country parks, rural areas and historic towns across the county. It generates a total business turnover of over £1 billion to the local economy and supports almost 20,000 jobs.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Warwickshire](/wiki/History_of_Warwickshire "History of Warwickshire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Warwickshire_Administrative_1832.png/250px-Warwickshire_Administrative_1832.png)](/wiki/File:Warwickshire_Administrative_1832.png)

Warwickshire in 1832

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/StratfordAvon20040717.jpg/250px-StratfordAvon20040717.jpg)](/wiki/File:StratfordAvon20040717.jpg)

[Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Kenilworth_Castle_gatehouse_landscape.jpg/250px-Kenilworth_Castle_gatehouse_landscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kenilworth_Castle_gatehouse_landscape.jpg)

[Kenilworth Castle](/wiki/Kenilworth_Castle "Kenilworth Castle")

Warwickshire came into being as a division of the kingdom of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") in the early 11th century. The first reference to Warwickshire was in 1001, as *Wæringscīr,* named after [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick"). The prefix *wara-* is the [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") [plural](/wiki/Plural "Plural") of the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") [noun](/wiki/Noun "Noun") *waru*, which means "those that care for, watch, guard, protect, or defend". It was used as an [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym") by both [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths")[[a]](#cite_note-35) and Jutes.[[b]](#cite_note-36) The suffix *-wick* is an [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") (*-wic*) for the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word for village, *vicus*. Near [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") are the villages of [Long Itchington](/wiki/Long_Itchington "Long Itchington") and [Bishop's Itchington](/wiki/Bishop%27s_Itchington "Bishop's Itchington") along the [River Itchen](/wiki/River_Itchen,_Warwickshire "River Itchen, Warwickshire").[[c]](#cite_note-38)

During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") Warwickshire was dominated by [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), at the time one of the most important cities in England because of its prominence in the textiles trade. Warwickshire played a key part in the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), with the [Battle of Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill") and other skirmishes taking place in the county. During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") Warwickshire became one of Britain's foremost industrial counties, with the large industrial cities of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") within its boundaries.

### Boundary changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Boundary changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/St_Mary%27s_church_in_Church_Street%2C_geograph_6862602_by_Steve_Daniels.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_church_in_Church_Street%2C_geograph_6862602_by_Steve_Daniels.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_church_in_Church_Street,_geograph_6862602_by_Steve_Daniels.jpg)

[Collegiate Church of St Mary, Warwick](/wiki/Collegiate_Church_of_St_Mary,_Warwick "Collegiate Church of St Mary, Warwick") from Church Street

* 1844: The [Counties (Detached Parts) Act](/wiki/Counties_(Detached_Parts)_Act_1844#Warwickshire "Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844") transferred a township to, and two parishes from, the county.
* 1888: Those parts of the town of [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire") lying in Warwickshire were ceded to Staffordshire.
* 1891: [Harborne](/wiki/Harborne "Harborne") became part of the County Borough of Birmingham and thus was transferred from Staffordshire to Warwickshire by the [Local Government Board's Provisional Order Confirmation (No. 13) Act 1891](/wiki/Local_Government_Board%27s_Provisional_Order_Confirmation_(No._13)_Act_1891 "Local Government Board's Provisional Order Confirmation (No. 13) Act 1891") ([54 & 55 Vict.](/wiki/54_%26_55_Vict. "54 & 55 Vict.") c. clxi) (local act).
* 1891: The district of [Balsall Heath](/wiki/Balsall_Heath "Balsall Heath"), which had originally constituted the most northerly part of the Parish of [King's Norton](/wiki/King%27s_Norton "King's Norton") in Worcestershire, was added to the [County Borough of Birmingham](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Birmingham "County Borough of Birmingham"), and therefore Warwickshire, on 1 October 1891.
* 1909: [Quinton](/wiki/Quinton,_Birmingham "Quinton, Birmingham") was formally removed from Worcestershire and incorporated into the County Borough of Birmingham, then in Warwickshire, on 9 November 1909.
* 1911: The [Urban District](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") [of Handsworth](/wiki/Handsworth,_West_Midlands "Handsworth, West Midlands"), in Staffordshire, and the [Rural District](/wiki/Rural_District "Rural District") [of Yardley](/wiki/Yardley,_Birmingham "Yardley, Birmingham") along with the greater part of the [Urban District of King's Norton and Northfield](/wiki/Urban_District_of_King%27s_Norton_and_Northfield "Urban District of King's Norton and Northfield"), both in Worcestershire, were absorbed into Birmingham, and thus Warwickshire, as part of the *Greater Birmingham Scheme* on 9 November 1911.
* 1928: [Perry Barr](/wiki/Perry_Barr "Perry Barr") [Urban District](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") was ceded to Birmingham from Staffordshire.
* 1931: The boundaries between Gloucestershire, Warwickshire, and Worcestershire were adjusted by the Provisional Order Confirmation (Gloucestershire, Warwickshire and Worcestershire) Act which transferred 26 parishes between the three counties, largely to eliminate [exclaves](/wiki/List_of_county_exclaves_in_England_and_Wales_1844_-_1974 "List of county exclaves in England and Wales 1844 - 1974"). The town of [Shipston-on-Stour](/wiki/Shipston-on-Stour "Shipston-on-Stour") was gained from Worcestershire and several villages, including [Long Marston](/wiki/Long_Marston,_Warwickshire "Long Marston, Warwickshire") and [Welford-on-Avon](/wiki/Welford-on-Avon "Welford-on-Avon"), from [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire"), while the parish of [Ipsley](/wiki/Ipsley "Ipsley") was ceded from [Alcester](/wiki/Alcester "Alcester") Rural District to [Redditch](/wiki/Redditch "Redditch") Urban District in Worcestershire.
* 1969: [Matchborough](/wiki/Matchborough "Matchborough") was ceded to Worcestershire as part of the [new town](/wiki/New_towns_in_the_United_Kingdom "New towns in the United Kingdom") development of Redditch.
* 1974: Under The [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), Birmingham, [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") and [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield") were ceded to the new [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)"), with Sutton Coldfield becoming part of Birmingham.

## Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Local government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Arms_of_Warwickshire_County_Council.svg/250px-Arms_of_Warwickshire_County_Council.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arms_of_Warwickshire_County_Council.svg)

The coat of arms of Warwickshire County Council

Like most English shire counties, Warwickshire has a two-tier [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") of a [county council](/wiki/County_council "County council"), and five [districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") each having a district or borough council. These districts are: [North Warwickshire](/wiki/North_Warwickshire "North Warwickshire"), [Nuneaton and Bedworth](/wiki/Nuneaton_and_Bedworth "Nuneaton and Bedworth"), [Rugby](/wiki/Borough_of_Rugby "Borough of Rugby"), [Stratford](/wiki/Stratford-on-Avon_(district) "Stratford-on-Avon (district)"), and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick_District "Warwick District") (see map). The county and district councils are responsible for providing different services.

[Atherstone](/wiki/Atherstone "Atherstone") is the headquarters of the North Warwickshire district, [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton") is headquarters of the Nuneaton and Bedworth District and [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa") is the headquarters of the Warwick district.

[Warwickshire County Council](/wiki/Warwickshire_County_Council "Warwickshire County Council"), based in [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") is elected every four years. The [election on 6 May 2021](/wiki/2021_Warwickshire_County_Council_election "2021 Warwickshire County Council election") resulted in a Conservative majority. The county council operates a [cabinet-style council](/wiki/Cabinet-style_council "Cabinet-style council"). The county council is made of 57 councillors, who decide upon the budget and appoints the council leader. The council leader selects 8 councillors and together they form the cabinet. The Leader assigns portfolios on which cabinet members make decisions. Key decisions are made by the whole cabinet while others are made only by the portfolio holders for relevant areas.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

In addition many small towns and villages have their own [town council](/wiki/Town_council "Town council") or [parish council](/wiki/Parish_councils_in_England "Parish councils in England") as the most local tier of local government.

Warwickshire is policed by the [Warwickshire Police](/wiki/Warwickshire_Police "Warwickshire Police"). The force is governed by the elected [Warwickshire Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/Warwickshire_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "Warwickshire Police and Crime Commissioner").

### Proposed local government reorganisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Proposed local government reorganisation")]

In August 2020 Warwickshire County Council put forward proposals for the five district and borough councils in the county to be abolished and replaced with a single county-wide [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England").[[37]](#cite_note-40) This prompted a backlash from the district and borough councils who commissioned their own report, which argued in favour of Warwickshire being split into two unitary authorities, one for the north of the county, covering the current districts of North Warwickshire, Nuneaton and Bedworth and Rugby, and one for the south of the county, covering Warwick and Stratford districts.[[38]](#cite_note-41) In September 2020, it was agreed that both proposals would be sent for consideration to the [Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Housing,_Communities_and_Local_Government "Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government").[[39]](#cite_note-42)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in Warwickshire](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Warwickshire "List of schools in Warwickshire")

In the state sector, children start school in the school year in which they turn five. They stay at primary school for seven years (although this varies even within the county, as some people have previously gone for four years and then spent another four years at a 'middle school') until they are eleven. Warwickshire is one of [36 local authorities](/wiki/List_of_grammar_schools_in_England "List of grammar schools in England") in England to still maintain the [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") system in two districts: Stratford-on-Avon and Rugby. In the final year of primary school, children are given the opportunity of sitting the [11-plus](/wiki/Eleven-plus_exam "Eleven-plus exam") exam to compete for a place at one of the 5 grammar schools: [Stratford-upon-Avon Grammar School for Girls](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_Grammar_School_for_Girls "Stratford-upon-Avon Grammar School for Girls"); [King Edward VI School](/wiki/King_Edward_VI_School,_Stratford-upon-Avon "King Edward VI School, Stratford-upon-Avon"), a boys' school from year 7–11 with a mixed Sixth-Form; [Lawrence Sheriff Grammar School for Boys](/wiki/Lawrence_Sheriff_School "Lawrence Sheriff School"); [Rugby High School for Girls](/wiki/Rugby_High_School_for_Girls "Rugby High School for Girls") and [Alcester Grammar School](/wiki/Alcester_Grammar_School "Alcester Grammar School") (mixed). The Warwickshire 11+ selection test consists of two papers, each containing a mixture of verbal reasoning, numerical reasoning and non-verbal reasoning multiple-choice questions.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

Warwickshire contains four colleges of further education: [North Warwickshire & Hinckley College](/wiki/North_Warwickshire_and_Hinckley_College "North Warwickshire and Hinckley College"), King Edward VI Sixth Form College (K.E.G.S) in Nuneaton, [Stratford-upon-Avon College](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_College "Stratford-upon-Avon College") and the [Warwickshire College Group](/wiki/WCG_(college) "WCG (college)") an institution made up of six main separate colleges that have merged (Leamington Centre, Rugby Centre, Moreton Morrell Centre, Pershore College, Henley-in-Arden Centre and the Trident Centre in Warwick).

There are also six independent senior schools within the county, namely: [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), [Warwick School](/wiki/Warwick_School "Warwick School"), [Princethorpe College](/wiki/Princethorpe_College "Princethorpe College"), [Kingsley School](/wiki/Kingsley_School "Kingsley School"), [Arnold Lodge School](/wiki/Arnold_Lodge_School "Arnold Lodge School") (both in [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa")), and [the King's High School For Girls](/wiki/The_King%27s_High_School_For_Girls "The King's High School For Girls") (in [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick")).

A number of the Warwickshire grammar and independent schools have historical significance. [King Edward VI School, Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/King_Edward_VI_School,_Stratford-upon-Avon "King Edward VI School, Stratford-upon-Avon") still uses 13th century school buildings and is the likely school of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") was founded in 1567 and [Warwick School](/wiki/Warwick_School "Warwick School") was founded c. 914 AD, which makes it the oldest surviving boys' school in the country. [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") is one of nine schools that were defined as the "great" English public schools by the [Public Schools Act 1868](/wiki/Public_Schools_Act_1868 "Public Schools Act 1868"), and is a member of the [Rugby Group](/wiki/Rugby_Group "Rugby Group"). [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), [Princethorpe College](/wiki/Princethorpe_College "Princethorpe College") and [Warwick School](/wiki/Warwick_School "Warwick School") are [HMC](/wiki/Headmasters%27_and_Headmistresses%27_Conference "Headmasters' and Headmistresses' Conference") schools, with the Headmaster from each school attending the [Headmasters' and Headmistresses' Conference](/wiki/Headmasters%27_and_Headmistresses%27_Conference "Headmasters' and Headmistresses' Conference").

There are no universities per se in Warwickshire, though the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick") forms part of the border with Warwickshire on the southern edge of the city of Coventry. Some areas of the University of Warwick are within the boundaries of Warwickshire including Lakeside Village and Warwick Business School[[41]](#cite_note-44) The university has a small campus near Wellesbourne which houses the Warwick Horticultural Research Centre and an Innovation Centre.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Roads")]

Several major [motorways](/wiki/Motorway "Motorway") run through Warwickshire; these are:

* The [M40 motorway](/wiki/M40_motorway "M40 motorway"), which connects [London](/wiki/London "London") to [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), runs through the centre of the county; it serves Leamington Spa, Warwick and Stratford.
* The [M6 motorway](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway"), which connects [North West England](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England") and the West Midlands to the [M1 motorway](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway") (and then on to London), runs through the north of Warwickshire; it serves Rugby, Nuneaton and Bedworth on its way to Birmingham.
* The [M69](/wiki/M69_motorway "M69 motorway") Coventry to Leicester motorway serves Nuneaton.
* Other motorways pass briefly through Warwickshire including the [M45](/wiki/M45_motorway "M45 motorway") (a short spur south of Rugby connecting to the M1), the southern end of the [M6 Toll](/wiki/M6_Toll "M6 Toll") and the [M42](/wiki/M42_motorway "M42 motorway"); it passes through the county at several points.

Other major trunk routes in Warwickshire include:

* [A45](/wiki/A45_road "A45 road") takes a route through Birmingham, Coventry and Rugby, then east into Northamptonshire
* [A46](/wiki/A46_road "A46 road") connects the M40 to the M6 via Warwick, Kenilworth and Coventry
* [A452](/wiki/A452_road "A452 road") Leamington to Birmingham route
* [A5](/wiki/A5_road_(Great_Britain) "A5 road (Great Britain)") passes through [Atherstone](/wiki/Atherstone "Atherstone") and then east of Nuneaton, and then east of Rugby, it marks the county boundary with Leicestershire.
* A444 goes through Nuneaton and Bedworth.

The West Coast Main Line at Rugby

#### Main line routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Main line routes")]

Two main railway lines pass through Warwickshire:

* The [Chiltern Main Line](/wiki/Chiltern_Main_Line "Chiltern Main Line") takes the former [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") route from [London Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone_station "Marylebone station") to [Birmingham Snow Hill](/wiki/Birmingham_Snow_Hill_railway_station "Birmingham Snow Hill railway station"). It passes through the centre of Warwickshire on a route similar to the M40 motorway and has stations at [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa_railway_station "Leamington Spa railway station"), [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick_railway_station "Warwick railway station"), [Warwick Parkway](/wiki/Warwick_Parkway_railway_station "Warwick Parkway railway station"), [Hatton](/wiki/Hatton_railway_station_(Warwickshire) "Hatton railway station (Warwickshire)") and [Lapworth](/wiki/Lapworth_railway_station "Lapworth railway station"). Services are provided by [Chiltern Railways](/wiki/Chiltern_Railways "Chiltern Railways") and [West Midlands Trains](/wiki/West_Midlands_Trains "West Midlands Trains") (Birmingham to Leamington only). There are also two branches off the Chiltern line: the [Leamington to Coventry](/wiki/Coventry_to_Leamington_Line "Coventry to Leamington Line") and [Leamington-Stratford](/wiki/Leamington_to_Stratford_Line "Leamington to Stratford Line") lines.
* The [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line") (WCML) runs through Warwickshire. At [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_railway_station "Rugby railway station"), the WCML splits into two parts: one runs west through to [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry_railway_station "Coventry railway station") and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham_New_Street_railway_station "Birmingham New Street railway station"), and the other the [Trent Valley Line](/wiki/Trent_Valley_Line "Trent Valley Line") runs north-west towards [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford_railway_station "Stafford railway station"), North West England and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). This section has stations at [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton_railway_station "Nuneaton railway station"), [Atherstone](/wiki/Atherstone_railway_station "Atherstone railway station") and [Polesworth](/wiki/Polesworth_railway_station "Polesworth railway station") (northbound services only). There is one branch off the WCML from [Nuneaton to Coventry](/wiki/Coventry_to_Nuneaton_Line "Coventry to Nuneaton Line"), with there are stations at [Bermuda Park](/wiki/Bermuda_Park_railway_station "Bermuda Park railway station"), [Bedworth](/wiki/Bedworth_railway_station "Bedworth railway station") and [Coventry Arena](/wiki/Coventry_Arena_railway_station "Coventry Arena railway station").

#### Other lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other lines")]

Other railway lines in Warwickshire include:

* Birmingham-Nuneaton section of the [Birmingham to Peterborough Line](/wiki/Birmingham_to_Peterborough_Line "Birmingham to Peterborough Line"), which continues east of Nuneaton towards [Leicester](/wiki/Leicester_railway_station "Leicester railway station") and [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough_railway_station "Peterborough railway station"). Nuneaton has direct services to Birmingham and Leicester on this line; there are two intermediate stations at [Water Orton](/wiki/Water_Orton_railway_station "Water Orton railway station") and [Coleshill](/wiki/Coleshill_Parkway_railway_station "Coleshill Parkway railway station") in the extreme north-west of the county.
* [North Warwickshire Line](/wiki/North_Warwickshire_Line "North Warwickshire Line") from Birmingham to [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_railway_station "Stratford-upon-Avon railway station"). This line used to continue southwards to [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham "Cheltenham"), but is now a dead-end branch. There is an intermediate station on this line at [Henley-in-Arden](/wiki/Henley-in-Arden_railway_station "Henley-in-Arden railway station") and at several small villages. Stratford also has direct rail services to London via the branch line to Warwick.

Between 1965 and 2018, the only major town in Warwickshire without a station was [Kenilworth](/wiki/Kenilworth "Kenilworth"). The Leamington to Coventry line passes through the town, but the station was closed as part of the [Beeching cuts](/wiki/Beeching_cuts "Beeching cuts"). [Kenilworth railway station](/wiki/Kenilworth_railway_station "Kenilworth railway station") was rebuilt and opened in April 2018,[[43]](#cite_note-46) with an hourly service to Coventry and to Leamington provided by [West Midlands Trains](/wiki/West_Midlands_Trains "West Midlands Trains").

#### High Speed 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: High Speed 2")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/HS2_construction_near_Leamington_Spa%2C_aerial_2021_%283%29%2C_geograph_6947416_by_Chris.jpg/250px-HS2_construction_near_Leamington_Spa%2C_aerial_2021_%283%29%2C_geograph_6947416_by_Chris.jpg)](/wiki/File:HS2_construction_near_Leamington_Spa,_aerial_2021_(3),_geograph_6947416_by_Chris.jpg)

HS2 construction near Leamington Spa in August 2021

The new [High Speed 2](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2") (HS2) line is a long-distance route that is being constructed through Warwickshire; however, there will be no stations in the county. It will pass south of [Southam](/wiki/Southam "Southam"), then between Kenilworth and Coventry, before running into the West Midlands towards Birmingham.[[44]](#cite_note-47)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Air")]

[Coventry Airport](/wiki/Coventry_Airport "Coventry Airport") is located in the Warwickshire village of [Baginton](/wiki/Baginton "Baginton").

### Canals and waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Canals and waterways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Oxford_Canal_from_Napton.jpg/250px-Oxford_Canal_from_Napton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oxford_Canal_from_Napton.jpg)

The Oxford Canal at [Napton-on-the-Hill](/wiki/Napton-on-the-Hill "Napton-on-the-Hill")

[Canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal") and navigable waterways in Warwickshire include:

* The [Coventry Canal](/wiki/Coventry_Canal "Coventry Canal"), which runs through the north of the county from [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") through [Bedworth](/wiki/Bedworth "Bedworth"), [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton"), [Atherstone](/wiki/Atherstone "Atherstone") and [Polesworth](/wiki/Polesworth "Polesworth"), and then onwards to [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_Staffordshire "Tamworth, Staffordshire").
* The [Ashby-de-la-Zouch Canal](/wiki/Ashby-de-la-Zouch_Canal "Ashby-de-la-Zouch Canal") passes briefly through Warwickshire from a junction with the Coventry Canal at [Bedworth](/wiki/Bedworth "Bedworth").
* The [Oxford Canal](/wiki/Oxford_Canal "Oxford Canal") runs from near Coventry, then eastwards around Rugby and through the rural south of the county towards [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford").
* The [Grand Union Canal](/wiki/Grand_Union_Canal "Grand Union Canal") runs through Leamington and Warwick, then onwards to Birmingham.
  + The restored [Saltisford Canal Arm](/wiki/Saltisford_Canal_Arm "Saltisford Canal Arm") is close to the centre of Warwick and is now a short branch of the Grand Union Canal. The arm is the remains of the original terminus of the Warwick and Birmingham Canal; it dates back to 1799.
* The [Stratford-upon-Avon Canal](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_Canal "Stratford-upon-Avon Canal") runs from the Grand Union west of Warwick to Stratford, where it joins the Avon.
* The [River Avon](/wiki/River_Avon,_Warwickshire "River Avon, Warwickshire") runs through Warwickshire on a south-west to north-east axis, running through Stratford, Warwick and Rugby. It is navigable for 47 miles (76 km) from the [River Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn") at [Tewkesbury](/wiki/Tewkesbury "Tewkesbury") to [Alveston](/wiki/Alveston,_Warwickshire "Alveston, Warwickshire") [weir](/wiki/Weir "Weir") just east of Stratford-upon-Avon, making it the only navigable river in Warwickshire.[[45]](#cite_note-ANT-48) There have been proposals to extend the Avon navigation 13 miles (21 km) to Warwick.[[46]](#cite_note-49) However, as of 2019, these plans look unlikely to proceed.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Television")]

The county is covered by [BBC West Midlands](/wiki/BBC_West_Midlands "BBC West Midlands") and [ITV Central](/wiki/ITV_Central "ITV Central") from its studios in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"). Television signals are received from either the Lark Stoke or [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield_transmitting_station "Sutton Coldfield transmitting station") TV transmitters.[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Radio")]

[BBC Local Radio](/wiki/BBC_Local_Radio "BBC Local Radio") for the county is served by [BBC CWR](/wiki/BBC_CWR "BBC CWR") which broadcast from its studios in [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"). However, the [North Warwickshire](/wiki/North_Warwickshire "North Warwickshire") area is served by [BBC Radio WM](/wiki/BBC_Radio_WM "BBC Radio WM"). County-wide commercial radio stations are [Capital Mid-Counties](/wiki/Capital_Mid-Counties "Capital Mid-Counties"), [Hits Radio Coventry & Warwickshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Coventry_%26_Warwickshire "Hits Radio Coventry & Warwickshire"), [Fresh (Coventry & Warwickshire)](/wiki/Fresh_(Coventry_%26_Warwickshire) "Fresh (Coventry & Warwickshire)"), [Heart West Midlands](/wiki/Heart_West_Midlands "Heart West Midlands") and [Greatest Hits Radio Midlands](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Midlands "Greatest Hits Radio Midlands").

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sport")]

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Warwickshire's rural roads, canal towpaths and historic towns are increasingly popular with cycling enthusiasts.[[50]](#cite_note-53) Its reputation as a major cycling destination has been bolstered in recent years having hosted a stage of the Women's Tour since 2016[[51]](#cite_note-54) and the Men's Tour of Britain in 2018 and 2019.[[52]](#cite_note-55)

In 2022, St Nicholas Park in Warwick hosted the *Elite Men's and Women's Road Race* as part of the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") that took place in Birmingham.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

### Association football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Association football")]

Warwickshire has no [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") clubs. As of the 2022–23 season, the highest-placed team is [Leamington](/wiki/Leamington_F.C. "Leamington F.C."), who play in the [National League North](/wiki/National_League_North "National League North"), the sixth tier of English football. A level below, in the [Southern Football League](/wiki/Southern_Football_League "Southern Football League") Premier Division Central, are [Nuneaton Borough](/wiki/Nuneaton_Borough_F.C. "Nuneaton Borough F.C.") and [Stratford Town](/wiki/Stratford_Town_F.C. "Stratford Town F.C."). Other clubs include [Rugby Town](/wiki/Rugby_Town_F.C. "Rugby Town F.C."), [Bedworth United](/wiki/Bedworth_United_F.C. "Bedworth United F.C."), [Southam United](/wiki/Southam_United_F.C. "Southam United F.C."), [Racing Club Warwick](/wiki/Racing_Club_Warwick_F.C. "Racing Club Warwick F.C."), [Coleshill Town](/wiki/Coleshill_Town_F.C. "Coleshill Town F.C."), [Atherstone Town](/wiki/Atherstone_Town_F.C. "Atherstone Town F.C.") and [Nuneaton Griff](/wiki/Nuneaton_Griff_F.C. "Nuneaton Griff F.C."); all of these are affiliated to the [Birmingham FA](/wiki/Birmingham_County_Football_Association "Birmingham County Football Association").

[Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), a [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") team, and Football League clubs [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") are located within the historic boundaries of Warwickshire; National League club [Solihull Moors](/wiki/Solihull_Moors_F.C. "Solihull Moors F.C.") and Southern League Division One Central club [Sutton Coldfield Town](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield_Town_F.C. "Sutton Coldfield Town F.C.") are also sited in this area.

### Parkrun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Parkrun")]

There are six Saturday morning 5 km parkruns in Warwickshire for all ages and abilities: Leamington, Stratford upon Avon, Rugby, Bedworth, Southam and Kingsbury. There are also three Sunday 2 km junior events at Stratford upon Avon, Rugby and Warwick.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

### Cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cricket")]

[Warwickshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Warwickshire_County_Cricket_Club "Warwickshire County Cricket Club") play at [Edgbaston Cricket Ground](/wiki/Edgbaston_Cricket_Ground "Edgbaston Cricket Ground"), Birmingham, which was historically part of Warwickshire. Notable English players for the side have been [Eric Hollies](/wiki/Eric_Hollies "Eric Hollies"), [M.J.K. Smith](/wiki/M.J.K._Smith "M.J.K. Smith"), [Bob Willis](/wiki/Bob_Willis "Bob Willis"), [Dennis Amiss](/wiki/Dennis_Amiss "Dennis Amiss"), [Jonathan Trott](/wiki/Jonathan_Trott "Jonathan Trott"), [Ian Bell](/wiki/Ian_Bell "Ian Bell"), [Moeen Ali](/wiki/Moeen_Ali "Moeen Ali") and [Chris Woakes](/wiki/Chris_Woakes "Chris Woakes"). Overseas players have included [Alvin Kallicharran](/wiki/Alvin_Kallicharran "Alvin Kallicharran"), [Rohan Kanhai](/wiki/Rohan_Kanhai "Rohan Kanhai"), [Brian Lara](/wiki/Brian_Lara "Brian Lara"), [Allan Donald](/wiki/Allan_Donald "Allan Donald") and [Shaun Pollock](/wiki/Shaun_Pollock "Shaun Pollock"). In 2014, the club partly severed its links to the county by renaming its [Twenty20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") side the *Birmingham Bears*, much to the chagrin of many supporters.[[55]](#cite_note-58)

Other grounds in modern-day Warwickshire which have hosted [first-class cricket matches](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") are:

* [Griff and Coton Ground](/wiki/Griff_and_Coton_Ground "Griff and Coton Ground"), [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton") – 26 matches (most recently 1980)
* [Arlington Avenue](/wiki/Leamington_Cricket_Club_Ground "Leamington Cricket Club Ground"), [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa") – 4 matches (most recently 1910)
* [Swan's Nest Lane](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon_Cricket_Club_Ground "Stratford-upon-Avon Cricket Club Ground"), [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon") – 3 matches (most recently 2005)
* [Weddington Road](/wiki/Nuneaton_Cricket_Club_Ground "Nuneaton Cricket Club Ground"), [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton") – 3 matches (most recently 1914)

### Gaelic sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Gaelic sports")]

The Warwickshire County Board of the [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") (GAA) (or Warwickshire GAA) is one of the [county boards](/wiki/GAA_county "GAA county") outside [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and is responsible for [Gaelic games](/wiki/Gaelic_games "Gaelic games") in Warwickshire. The county board is also responsible for the Warwickshire inter-county teams. They play their home games at [Páirc na hÉireann](/wiki/P%C3%A1irc_na_h%C3%89ireann,_West_Midlands "Páirc na hÉireann, West Midlands").

### Polo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Polo")]

The [Dallas Burston Polo Club](/wiki/Dallas_Burston_Polo_Club "Dallas Burston Polo Club") is a six-pitch [polo](/wiki/Polo_(sport) "Polo (sport)") club located near [Southam](/wiki/Southam "Southam").

### Water polo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Water polo")]

Warwick Water Polo club play in the Midland League, and train in Warwick, Banbury and Coventry.[[56]](#cite_note-59)

## Freedom of the county

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Freedom of the county")]

In March 2014 the freedom of the county was bestowed on the [Royal Regiment of Fusiliers](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Fusiliers "Royal Regiment of Fusiliers"). The honour was officially bestowed following a parade through [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") on 6 June 2014.[[57]](#cite_note-60)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Warwickshire&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: People")]

Further information: [Category:People from Warwickshire](/wiki/Category:People_from_Warwickshire "Category:People from Warwickshire")

Warwickshire was the birthplace of [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") from [Stratford-upon-Avon](/wiki/Stratford-upon-Avon "Stratford-upon-Avon"). Road signs at the county boundary describe Warwickshire as "Shakespeare's County". The county has produced figures such as [Aleister Crowley](/wiki/Aleister_Crowley "Aleister Crowley") (from [Leamington Spa](/wiki/Leamington_Spa "Leamington Spa")), [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot") and [Ken Loach](/wiki/Ken_Loach "Ken Loach") (from [Nuneaton](/wiki/Nuneaton "Nuneaton")), [Rupert Brooke](/wiki/Rupert_Brooke "Rupert Brooke") (from [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby,_Warwickshire "Rugby, Warwickshire")), and [Michael Drayton](/wiki/Michael_Drayton "Michael Drayton") (from [Hartshill](/wiki/Hartshill "Hartshill")). The poet [Philip Larkin](/wiki/Philip_Larkin "Philip Larkin") lived in Warwick (born in nearby Coventry). Folk musician [Nick Drake](/wiki/Nick_Drake "Nick Drake") lived and died in [Tanworth-in-Arden](/wiki/Tanworth-in-Arden "Tanworth-in-Arden"). [Frank Whittle](/wiki/Frank_Whittle "Frank Whittle") the inventor of the jet engine was born in Coventry and was closely associated with Warwickshire, growing up in Leamington Spa, and carrying out much of his work at Rugby.[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[59]](#cite_note-62)
